era_context: The Roman Republic continues its political decline. Mithridates VI remains dominant in the eastern Mediterranean. In China, the Han Dynasty under Xuandi is in its consolidation phase. The Parthian Empire controls Central Asian trade routes. This period marks the final decade before the outbreak of the First Mithridatic War and the First Roman Civil War.

events.title: Antiochus I Philometor of Syria dies; succession crisis in Seleucid Empire
events.description: Antiochus I Philometor, King of the Seleucid Empire, dies around this time (exact date uncertain). His death triggers a succession crisis that weakens Seleucid power and creates opportunities for neighboring powers, including Mithridates VI and Rome, to expand their influence. The Seleucid Empire's decline accelerates during this period.
key_figures: Antiochus I Philometor (Seleucid King, died c. 96 BCE)
events.certainty_note: Antiochus I Philometor's death is dated to around 96 BCE, but the exact year is uncertain. Different sources give slightly different dates. The dating is approximate.
cross_references: Part of Seleucid decline (-200 to -63 BCE)
Leads to further fragmentation of Seleucid power

events.title: Roman political instability; no major documented events
events.description: The Roman Republic continues its trajectory of political instability. No major events are specifically documented for 96 BCE in surviving sources. The year represents the broader context of Republican decline.
events.certainty_note: No specific events are documented for 96 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Antiochus I Philometor's death is disputed. Some sources place it in 95 BCE or 97 BCE rather than 96 BCE. The exact year of his death is uncertain, and different ancient sources give conflicting dates. Modern scholarship has not reached consensus on the precise year.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 96 BCE is sparse. The only potentially significant event (Antiochus I's death) is itself uncertain in dating. Most information about this year comes from later compilations rather than contemporary sources. The year is known primarily through its position within broader historical trends. The sparsity of documentation is typical for this period.

graph_edges.note: Seleucid weakness contributed to regional instability that affected Rome's eastern interests
